name: Koka
summary: Microsoft Research functional language tracking side effects in function types via row-polymorphic effect types and type-directed compilation strategies (such as selective CPS) driven by those effect rows. Canonical production example of effect handlers as first-class language structure.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Koka tracks side effects in function types and supports effect handlers as a typed, composable mechanism, using row-polymorphic effect types and type-directed compilation strategies (such as selective CPS) driven by those effect rows.

Every Koka function arrow carries a row-polymorphic effect set, so purity, exceptions, async, divergence, and user-defined operations are visible to inference, to handler typing, and to selective-CPS lowering. This is the canonical production example of "effects as first-class structure in the source".

sections.title: Row-polymorphic effect types
sections.content:
Function types in Koka have shape `A -> <effects> B` — the angle-brackets contain a row of effects. A pure function has empty effects (`<>`); an exception-throwing function has `<exn>`; an async function might have `<async, exn>`. Row variables (`<exn|e>`) abstract over "the rest of the effect set", letting higher-order combinators be effect-polymorphic.

Effect inference unifies effect rows alongside type unification. Programmers usually omit effects in source; the compiler infers and prints them in error messages and signatures. The discipline scales to user-defined operations: a custom `<state<S>>` effect declared by the programmer participates in inference exactly like built-in effects.

sources.label: Koka type-directed compilation of row-typed algebraic effects

sections.title: Selective CPS compilation
sections.content: Koka's compilation strategy uses the inferred effect row to drive selective CPS: pure code stays direct-style; effect-performing code is CPS-transformed so handlers can capture and resume it. The 2021 Xie-Leijen "generalized evidence passing" work extends the discipline so multi-handler interactions compose without runtime overhead beyond what the underlying effect operations require. Full lowering treatment lives in `data/effect-handler-lowering`.

sources.label: Koka type-directed compilation of row-typed algebraic effects
